cards.title: Anti-Patterns as Recurring Failure Modes
cards.teaser: Anti-patterns aren't mistakes — they're patterns that look correct in the short term and become expensive in the medium or long term.

runs.text: Anti-patterns share a characteristic: they solve the immediate problem quickly and cheaply, and the cost only becomes visible later — when the table has grown, when the team has grown, when the pipeline has been running for 18 months.
This chapter catalogs the most common ones observed across data teams. They're not exotic edge cases — they show up in nearly every team that builds data systems under time pressure.
Knowing them serves two purposes:

items.text: recognizing the pattern in a system you've inherited, so you can prioritize the remediation.
avoiding the pattern in a system you're building, so you don't create the same debt.

runs.text: The anti-patterns below are not things people do ignorantly — they're things that seem like fine decisions under the pressure of a deadline, with a team of two, with a table of 100K rows. The pain arrives later, when the context has changed.
Every anti-pattern in this chapter has been "the right call" in some context. The failure mode is applying it in contexts where it isn't.
Like technical debt in application code — not wrong per se; wrong when you don't account for the interest accumulating.

cards.title: Small-Files Explosion
cards.teaser: A thousand files that each hold one row are worse than one file that holds a thousand rows. Streaming writes and partition over-granularity are the common causes.

runs.text: The
small-files problem
occurs when a data lake accumulates millions of tiny files (under 1 MB), degrading query performance and inflating metadata operations.
How it happens:

items.text: Kafka consumers flush to S3 after each batch of 100 events, producing thousands of small files per hour.
on a table that receives 100 rows per minute creates 1,440 partitions per day, each with 100 rows.
every CDC event becomes a small Delta file before compaction runs.
a job that dies halfway through has written 50 small files that were never compacted.

runs.text: Consequences:

items.text: Spark opens each file separately; listing and opening 10,000 files takes longer than reading one 1 GB file with the same total data.
S3 LIST operations are rate-limited. A prefix with 10 million objects causes throttling.
per-object billing on some tiers; high request costs at massive file counts.

runs.text: Solutions:

items.text: (Iceberg), or a Spark coalesce job periodically.
in streaming pipelines, buffer events for 5 minutes before flushing to produce reasonably-sized files.
daily partitions instead of hourly if queries rarely filter to sub-day.

runs.text: Without scheduled compaction, a Kafka-to-Delta pipeline will accumulate small files continuously. Set up OPTIMIZE as a daily maintenance job from day one.
Like having a million sticky notes instead of a notebook — same information, much harder to find anything and much harder to carry around.

cards.title: Monolithic Transforms
cards.teaser: One giant SQL query or Python script that does everything — extract, join, clean, aggregate, and load — is fast to write and slow to debug, test, and modify.

runs.text: A
monolithic transform
combines multiple logical steps into a single, undifferentiated operation. A 500-line SQL query that joins 10 tables, applies business logic, and produces an output in one shot.
Why it happens: writing one query is faster than writing five. Under deadline pressure, the monolith gets shipped.
Why it hurts:

items.text: there's no way to test the intermediate steps independently. A bug in the join logic is buried in a wall of SQL.
when the output is wrong, there's no intermediate state to inspect.
any change to one part of the query risks breaking another. Engineers learn to fear touching it.
slow sections can't be profiled independently.
the engineer who wrote it carries all the context; when they leave, the query becomes opaque.

runs.text: What to do instead:

items.text: split the monolith into a sequence of intermediate tables or CTEs with clear names. Each stage can be tested and inspected independently.
in dbt, each transformation step is a separate model, tested independently, with documented lineage.
even within a single SQL query, named CTEs are more readable and testable than nested subqueries.

runs.text: The test for whether a transform is too large: can you describe what each section does in one sentence without referencing other sections? If not, split it.
Like a function that's 500 lines long — correct in the narrow sense, impossible to maintain, and the source of every subtle bug going forward.

cards.title: Untested SQL
cards.teaser: A SQL transform with no tests is a liability. The next schema change, volume change, or logic change will break it silently — and downstream consumers will find out before you do.

runs.text: Most data engineering pipelines include SQL transforms that have no automated tests. The transforms were written, manually verified once at the time, and deployed. From then on, they're assumed to be correct until someone notices otherwise.
Why SQL goes untested:

items.text: Testing SQL is less ergonomic than testing application code. There's no standard test framework with the mindshare of pytest or JUnit.
Manual verification feels sufficient — "I checked the output, it looks right."
The table has 10 rows in development. In production, with 10 million rows, behavior can differ.

runs.text: What breaks untested SQL:

items.text: a column rename nulls out a field the query relied on.
new data has NULLs in a column that was previously always populated. Aggregations now return unexpected results.
a join produces unexpected duplicates that the original data didn't have.
a requirement changes but only the application is updated; the SQL still uses the old logic.

runs.text: Testing approaches:

items.text: — four tests that catch the majority of common SQL failures.
SQL queries that return 0 rows when correct; any returned row is a test failure.
validate output tables after each transform run.
tools like dbt-unit-testing allow SQL logic testing on minimal mock datasets.

runs.text: The "I verified it once" argument fails the first time the source schema changes, which happens within 6 months for most pipelines. Tests catch that; memory doesn't.
Like deploying backend code without tests because "I manually tested it" — fine until the first edge case or dependency change.

cards.title: Manual Backfills
cards.teaser: Running backfills by hand — editing job parameters, running commands, watching output — is slow, error-prone, and doesn't scale. Pipelines should be backfillable without manual steps.

runs.text: A
manual backfill
means re-running a pipeline over historical data through a process that requires human intervention — editing config files, running shell commands, watching progress, re-running failures manually.
Why it happens: building proper backfill infrastructure takes time; the first backfill is urgent; you do it manually. The pattern solidifies.
Why it's a problem:

items.text: manually backfilling 30 days of data is slow; backfilling 365 days is a project.
manually specifying date ranges for each run introduces off-by-one errors and missed dates.
a manual backfill that ran three weeks ago isn't recorded in any system; the next person doesn't know it happened.
the on-call engineer who needs to recover a month of data after an incident shouldn't be running shell scripts for 8 hours.

runs.text: What well-designed pipelines enable:

items.text: Airflow's backfill command reruns a DAG over a date range automatically.
each day's backfill run is safe to re-run if it fails partway through.
the date is passed as a parameter by the orchestrator, not hardcoded in config.

explanation.code:
# Airflow backfill — no manual steps required
airflow dags backfill orders_daily_etl \
  --start-date 2026-01-01 \
  --end-date 2026-01-31

runs.text: Design every pipeline for backfill on day one. The test: can a new team member trigger a 30-day backfill without reading your notes? If not, the pipeline isn't complete.
Like a database migration you can only run manually — it works once, it's a nightmare the second time, and the third time is a 2am incident.

cards.title: Schema Drift Handling by Null
cards.teaser: When a source column disappears or changes type, pipelines that silently pass NULL downstream instead of alerting are hiding a data quality failure as a data value.

runs.text: Schema drift handling by null

is the pattern where a pipeline, on encountering a source column that has been renamed, dropped, or changed type, writes NULL to the destination column for affected rows — without alerting, without failing, without any visible signal.
Why it happens: pipelines are built to be resilient to missing data. NULL is the "safe" default. The engineer didn't anticipate this specific column disappearing.
Why it's dangerous:

items.text: NULL is a valid business value for many columns. NULL in
might mean "no email provided" — or it might mean "the source changed the column name and the pipeline silently failed."
No alert fires. Dashboards start showing incomplete data. Analysts assume the data is correct. Decisions are made on wrong numbers.
Discovery is typically downstream: "why are our email open rates zero this week?"

runs.text: Better alternatives:

items.text: fail the pipeline when the source schema doesn't match the expected schema. The failure is visible and actionable.
validate the schema before writing; route non-conforming records to a quarantine table rather than writing NULL.
for streaming pipelines, a schema registry rejects events that don't conform to the registered schema.
even if silently passing NULL, alert when the NULL rate for a column spikes above a threshold.

runs.text: A pipeline that silently passes NULL for unexpected source changes is worse than a pipeline that fails loudly. Failure is visible; silent NULL is invisible.
Like a build that compiles with warnings instead of errors — it "works," but the warnings are telling you something broke and you're choosing not to see it.
